summary: Chloroquine causes eye-related side effects through accumulation in the macula affecting RPE; it leads to parafoveal thinning and loss of photoreceptor integrity as seen via OCT and FAF imaging techniques without involving specific antiviral actions against retroviruses. The primary concern for clinicians treating patients with malaria using chloroquine should be monitoring their eyesight due to these direct toxicological impacts rather than any indirect immunomodulatory benefits unrelated to anti-HIV activity. To summarize concisely while focusing only on clinically significant aspects directly linked to patient care regarding potential vision impairment from chloroquine use, we highlight its known ophthalmic adverse effect profile driven solely by drug metabolism within sensitive ocular tissues irrespective of broader therapeutic intentions beyond just combating infectious diseases caused specifically by viruses such as those belonging to Retrovirus family including Human Immunodeficiency Virus type one & two among others mentioned here explicitly but not causally connected otherwise under normal circumstances where prophylactic measures would include regular visual assessments when prescribing high doses over prolonged periods especially if pre-existing conditions exist predisposing individuals towards developing more severe complications faster compared to general population averages exposed similarly albeit less frequently overall statistically speaking across
